cuisine: Indonesian (Javanese / Betawi origin — also common in Malaysia)
food_subgroup: Beef braised in sweet soy sauce (kecap manis) with nutmeg and cloves

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Semur Daging. Closest parent is sweet soy-braised beef stew.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef braised in sweet soy sauce with spices (Indonesian semur daging style)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Semur Daging serving is approximately 200g: 120g beef cubes with 80g of the rich dark kecap manis sauce. Served alongside steamed rice (not included).

nutrient_source.title: Semur Daging — FAO/INFOODS SEA Table
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table is the primary source for Semur Daging. High sugar from kecap manis (palm sugar-based sweet soy) is the key nutritional distinguisher. Values per 100g represent beef + kecap manis sauce composite.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI driven by kecap manis (sweet soy sauce) which uses palm sugar. Palm sugar has lower GI (~35–55) than cane sugar. The protein from beef moderates carbohydrate absorption. Estimated GI ~48–55. Per-200g serving delivers approximately 17g carbohydrate; GL per serving ~8.8 (moderate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from kecap manis (soy sauce base) is significant. Kecap manis contains approximately 1,000–1,500mg sodium per 100g. A 200g serving of semur daging delivers approximately 1,040mg sodium — 43% of the daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should monitor semur daging consumption. The kecap manis is the primary sodium source — request reduced kecap manis in preparation. Limit to once per week. The distinctive flavour depends on kecap manis so significant substitution is difficult.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Kecap manis contributes 6.5g sugar per 100g (palm sugar-based). A 200g serving delivers 13g sugar with GL ~8.8 — moderate. Palm sugar has lower GI than refined cane sugar, giving semur daging a more moderate glycaemic impact than dishes using regular sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes can eat semur daging with moderate portion control. The palm sugar-based kecap manis has a more favourable GI than regular sugar. Pair with controlled rice. The beef provides zinc (3.5mg/100g) which supports glucose metabolism.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lower saturated fat than coconut milk curries (2.8g/100g). High zinc (3.5mg/100g) from beef supports immune function. Cloves in the spice base (eugenol) and nutmeg have anti-inflammatory properties. The kecap manis sugar may promote inflammation with very frequent consumption.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Semur daging is a relatively good choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — lower saturated fat, high zinc, and anti-inflammatory spices (cloves, nutmeg, black pepper). Limit to 2 times per week. The kecap manis sugar requires monitoring.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Semur Daging is an Indonesian slow-braised beef dish with distinctive Dutch colonial influence — 'semur' derives from the Dutch 'smoor' (braise). Beef cubes are braised in kecap manis (Indonesian palm sugar-sweetened soy sauce) with warming whole spices: nutmeg, cloves, black pepper, and crispy fried shallots. The kecap manis gives the dish a characteristic dark, glossy, sweet-savory sauce with deep umami. It is a common Javanese home-cooking staple and festive Betawi dish. The use of kecap manis (versus Chinese soy sauce) distinguishes it from Chinese braised beef — kecap manis is considerably sweeter and lower in sodium than regular soy sauce.

confidence_reason: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table primary source. Values consistent with known kecap manis-braised beef composition.
notes: Kecap manis (sweet soy sauce) contains palm sugar — which has lower GI than cane sugar, giving semur daging a more moderate glycaemic profile than its high sugar content might suggest. Zinc (3.5mg/100g) from beef is a notable nutrient. Gluten concern: kecap manis contains fermented soy/wheat — not gluten-free. The Dutch colonial influence on Indonesian cuisine is uniquely represented in semur.
